hooks.command: CMD=$(jq -r '.tool_input.command // ""' 2>/dev/null); if echo "$CMD" | grep -qE 'git commit'; then STAGED=$(git diff --name-only --cached 2>/dev/null); if echo "$STAGED" | grep -qE '^(agents|runtime|interfaces)/'; then if ! echo "$STAGED" | grep -q '^tests/'; then echo '{"hookSpecificOutput":{"hookEventName":"PreToolUse","permissionDecision":"deny","permissionDecisionReason":"Behavior files in agents/, runtime/, or interfaces/ are staged with no test files. Add or update tests for the changed behavior before committing. Run: npm test"}}'; fi; fi; fi; true

FILE=$(jq -r '.tool_input.file_path // ""' 2>/dev/null); echo "$FILE" | grep -q '\.html$' && echo '{"hookSpecificOutput":{"hookEventName":"PreToolUse","permissionDecision":"deny","permissionDecisionReason":"HTML files must be generated by generateDesignPreview() in runtime/html-renderer.ts -- never hand-written. The renderer is the agent. Call the function and write the result via fs.writeFileSync() in a script."}}' || true

FILE=$(jq -r '.tool_input.file_path // ""' 2>/dev/null); echo "$FILE" | grep -q 'agentic-health360' && echo '{"hookSpecificOutput":{"hookEventName":"PreToolUse","permissionDecision":"deny","permissionDecisionReason":"agentic-health360 is a read-only customer repo. Fix the platform parser instead of modifying the customer repo. See Principle 3a in CLAUDE.md."}}' || true

file=$(jq -r '.tool_input.file_path // empty' 2>/dev/null); if echo "$file" | grep -qE '/(agents|runtime|interfaces)/[^/]+\.ts$'; then echo '{"hookSpecificOutput": {"hookSpecificOutput": "PostToolUse", "additionalContext": "[CLAUDE.md Definition of Done] Modified agent/runtime/interface file. Confirm ALL that apply:
- AGENTS.md updated (agent persona/capabilities changed)
- SYSTEM_ARCHITECTURE.md updated (data flow or behavior changed)
- BACKLOG.md updated (move completed item to Completed section)
- DECISIONS.md updated (if any solo-team shortcut taken)
- .env.example updated (if new WorkspaceConfig field added)"}}'; fi

hooks.prompt:
You enforce architectural rules for the agentic-sdlc codebase. Examine the tool input in $ARGUMENTS.

Step 1: Check the file path. If it does NOT match agents/, runtime/, or interfaces/ directories, respond ONLY with: {}

Step 2: For matching files, check the written code for these FIVE violations:

VIOLATION A — BRITTLE ROUTING: A regex or keyword pattern used to classify or route user-facing behavior (message intent, render/preview requests, approval detection) without a '// NARROW:' comment that names the failure case and the durable fix.

VIOLATION B — PROMPT-RULE-ONLY ENFORCEMENT: A trust-critical behavior (the agent must never give up, must always save a draft, must not ask permission) enforced ONLY by text in a system prompt string, with no corresponding platform-level guard in message.ts or a runtime file.

VIOLATION C — TRUNCATION: Using .slice(0, N) or .substring(0, N) on text retrieved from GitHub or a spec/document file, instead of relevance filtering via a Haiku API call. Truncation silently loses content; relevance filtering preserves what matters.

VIOLATION D — AGENT BYPASS: A function or code path that reads from GitHub API, reads files from disk, or reads from the conversation store AND returns user-facing text output WITHOUT calling client.messages.create() or equivalent Claude API call. This loses agent conversation context and produces stale output.

VIOLATION E — HARDCODED PRODUCT COORDINATE: A string literal in agents/, runtime/, or interfaces/ that hardcodes a specific product name (e.g. 'health360'), GitHub repo slug (e.g. 'agentic-health360'), Slack channel name (e.g. '#feature-onboarding'), or spec file path (e.g. 'onboarding.design.md') that should instead come from WorkspaceConfig.

VIOLATION F — FREE-TEXT KEYWORD CLASSIFICATION: A function that accepts a string parameter representing user-authored free text (named 'message', 'msg', 'userMessage', 'text', or similar) and uses .includes(), .startsWith(), .toLowerCase(), or a regex to make a boolean or categorical classification decision, instead of calling client.messages.create() with a Haiku prompt. Humans express the same intent in infinite ways — keyword matching always has gaps ('can we ship?' vs 'ready to hand off?', 'good to go?' vs 'is this ready?'). The only correct approach for classifying human free-text intent is LLM classification (Haiku). Flag any new function that takes a free-text string and returns a classification using string operations.

IMPORTANT: Only flag violations in the NEW code being written (the new_string or content field in the tool input). Do NOT flag pre-existing code that surrounds the change.

If any violation is found, respond ONLY with valid JSON (this surfaces as a warning I must resolve before closing the task — it does NOT stop my current edit loop):
{"additionalContext": "[CLAUDE.md VIOLATION] <letter>: <one sentence describing the specific issue in the new code>. Durable fix: <what platform-level code or config should handle this instead>."}

If no violations found, respond ONLY with: {}
